name: Root Beer Water Kefir
subtitle: Classic root beer flavour with sassafras, wintergreen, and vanilla — a probiotic nostalgia trip
blrNote: Root beer flavouring is the tricky part in India. Root beer extract (McCormick or similar) is available on Amazon India for ₹300–500. Alternatively, build the flavour from components: vanilla extract (Nature's Basket), sarsaparilla root (Ayurvedic stores or Amazon — called 'anantmool' in Hindi), star anise (KR Market), and wintergreen oil (rare — try essential oil suppliers on Amazon). At BLR temps, 24 hours of 2F is sufficient.

IN.where: Homemade from water kefir grains. See water-kefir-basic recipe.

ingredients.name: Root beer extract
substitutions: DIY blend: 1/2 tsp vanilla + 1 star anise + 1 small piece sarsaparilla root + tiny drop wintergreen oil
Sarsaparilla root (anantmool) steeped in hot water

IN.where: Amazon India (McCormick, Zatarain's). ₹300–500. Or build from components — sarsaparilla root (anantmool) from Ayurvedic stores.
Nature's Basket, Amazon India, baking supply shops.
Any kirana store or KR Market spice section.

equipment.name: Plastic or nylon mesh strainer
equipment.notes: To strain out star anise and any solid ingredients.

tldr: Add root beer extract (or a DIY spice blend), vanilla, and brown sugar to finished water kefir, seal and ferment 24–48 hours. A nostalgic, creamy, probiotic root beer.

steps.title: Prepare base water kefir
steps.instruction: Make a batch of plain water kefir following the water-kefir-basic recipe. Strain out the grains. You need about 750ml of finished plain water kefir.
tips: A sweeter 1F works best for root beer — the sweetness carries the complex botanical flavours.
Reserve grains in fresh sugar water for your next batch.
steps.checkpoint: 750ml of strained, room-temperature plain water kefir

steps.title: Build the root beer flavour
steps.instruction: Add 1 tsp root beer extract, 1 tsp vanilla extract, 2 tbsp brown sugar, and 1 star anise to your swing-top bottle. If using the DIY method instead of extract: steep 5g sarsaparilla root in 100ml hot water for 15 minutes, strain, cool, and add along with vanilla, sugar, and star anise.
tips: Root beer extract is the easiest path — it contains a balanced blend of sassafras, wintergreen, and other botanicals.
The DIY method is more work but gives you complete control over the flavour profile.
Star anise adds a licorice note that's essential to root beer character.
Brown sugar gives the dark colour and molasses notes root beer is known for.
steps.checkpoint: Root beer flavourings and sugar in the bottle

steps.title: Second ferment (2F)
steps.instruction: Pour the strained water kefir over the root beer flavouring. Seal tightly. Place in a warm spot away from direct sunlight for 24–48 hours. Burp every 12 hours.
tips: The sugar will fuel carbonation. The botanical extracts don't contribute fermentable sugars.
At BLR temps, 24 hours usually gives good carbonation.
The colour will darken from the brown sugar and root beer extract.
The aroma should be unmistakably root beer — vanilla, sassafras, wintergreen.
steps.checkpoint: Bottle pressurized, dark brown, root beer aroma

steps.title: Strain and serve
steps.instruction: Open carefully. Strain out the star anise. Serve over ice — or go full American and add a scoop of vanilla ice cream for a probiotic root beer float. Refrigerate for up to 7 days.
tips: Root beer float: scoop of vanilla ice cream + this kefir = probiotic nostalgia.
The foam on top is natural from fermentation — it mimics the creamy head of commercial root beer.
Excellent cold, straight from the fridge. The vanilla becomes more pronounced when chilled.
This is a great conversation piece — most people don't believe it's homemade and probiotic.
steps.checkpoint: Dark, creamy, fizzy probiotic root beer

culturalContext.story: Root beer is a quintessentially American beverage, originally made by colonial-era pharmacists from sassafras bark, sarsaparilla root, and various botanicals. The original versions were actually fermented — making this water kefir root beer historically authentic. Before commercialization, every household and drugstore had their own root beer recipe.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Sassafras tea was one of the first exports from the New World to Europe. Indigenous peoples of North America had been using sassafras and sarsaparilla in beverages for centuries before European contact. Root beer as a carbonated drink emerged in the 1840s, and was naturally fermented until the 20th century when artificial carbonation took over.
culturalContext.significance: This recipe returns root beer to its fermented origins. It's a reminder that most 'sodas' were originally fermented beverages — commercial carbonation was the disruption, not the tradition. Making root beer with water kefir is historically authentic.
culturalContext.funFact: The US FDA banned sassafras oil from foods in 1960 due to safrole content, which was linked to cancer in rats at very high doses. Modern root beer extract uses safrole-free sassafras flavouring — so your probiotic root beer is perfectly safe.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Root beer extract availability in India
thingsToAccountFor.description: Root beer is not part of Indian food culture, so the extract can be hard to find locally. Amazon India is your best bet. Alternatively, use sarsaparilla root (anantmool), available at Ayurvedic stores, as the base for a DIY approach.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Star anise is potent
thingsToAccountFor.description: One star anise is enough for 750ml. More will make the drink taste too heavily of licorice. Remove it after 24 hours if doing a 48-hour ferment.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Not recommended for flavoured water kefir.
tips: Freeze into root beer popsicles or ice cubes for root beer floats

variations.name: Root Beer Float Kefir
variations.description: Serve the finished root beer kefir with a scoop of vanilla ice cream. Classic American treat, now with probiotics.

variations.name: Sarsaparilla Kefir
variations.description: Use only sarsaparilla root (anantmool) and vanilla — skip the extract. A simpler, more herbal version closer to traditional root beer.
